sentences.text: Yes.
When I got to the bottom of this C-lager called where we was in, I recognized there is like-- I can't compare it to anything.
It's just full length of a-- a barrack was empty.
But in the-- on the floor is some wooden or, or, or stone long like low tables and holes in it.
And we, people was sitting back to back on that.
That was toilet.
But I didn't know what it is.
And we wasn't eating so long ago, drinking when we needed.
Why these people was there I have no idea.
But that was the very last, it was the end of the C-lager, that's a row of two side at that street of barracks.
And I went in there, huge number of people was sitting, I think with two rows of back to back, you know.
People were sitting on that.
And I was shouting top of my voice, Mommy, Mommy, Mrs.
Hochhauser.
And all of the suddenly, I recognized [SOBBING] I was standing front of my mother.
But I didn't recognized her, because we went in in normal clothes and hair.
And there was no hair and just a rag.
And we just broke down and cried, because she didn't recognize me either.
Just recognized what I was shouting for.
I was only 12.
Now, I was [? 13 ?] in January.
And that was sometime in March or April.
I don't know when, but was not very long after.
It was really terrible, so I never went back to that barrack where I was supposed to go I went to the barrack where she said she supposed to go to.
So in-between these barracks had a, a place where was-- people could drink water, called washroom.
But there was like long half-cut pipes, sort of, and I can't, it's like large pipes cut in half, you know, and was fixed on-- higher.
And was load of taps in intervals where the water was come in, so where people could wash their selves and, and drink water.
But was no soap, obviously, and no cup to drink from.
You use the hand, and how you can wash yourself.
And there is everybody there, load of people.
And I recognized there was a, a, a low area which was like a coal bunker, you know, used to being in England keeping coal for winter.
You know, the sort of a flat-top bunkers to keep this coal in it.
Now, this one had one side empty, not open.
I mean, not, no gate or anything.
It's open, but empty inside.
And I see them taking out these barrels in it with some metal barrels, like dustbins.
And I'm told we are going to have food.
And they brought it in these dustbins, which was very gray.
I was thinking about it.
It's called [GERMAN].
What does that mean?
I don't know.
It was some gray, gooey something.
And we were so hungry, we was eating it.
Who did you see taking these barrels out?
Those women who came from Poland before we did.
And they had hair.
I don't know when they came, but their hair was grown.
And they had some reasonable clothes and these were.
Now these people all had tattoo.
So I have a, because I haven't got any tattoo of my own, I have a strong feeling they only put tattoo on women who was this sort or man-- because later on I met men who got tattoo.
I think who was this sort look strong enough to do some work and might be survive longer.
So those people will go first to the gas and then to the ovens who looked the thinnest and less fit.
So you see, I, I-- I'm just lucky I survived, because they never put tattoo on me.
How did you know that they were Polish women who--
Because they were speaking Polish language.
And some of these Polish women did speak a little Hungarian as well.
So that they said, you know, where they-- where they come from.

And where these is, some of these came from the names of the towns, which is family obviously, is town, like Cracow and, oh, I don't know, I, I recognized the names of those towns as belong to Poland.
Anyway, they said they are Polish.
They said polska, is mean Polish in Polish.
And I guessed it.
And every barrack had two women who was responsible for us.
I don't know how they was, anyway-- to make sure we stay put where.
Anyway, course of the time we was in this place.
Forever and ever had to stand on this side of the barrack, five by five behind one another for hours and hours.
These German soldiers came up and down, and kept on, I'm, I'm sure, watched it our numbers is there.
I mean, where could we go?
Anyway end of the evening, they was given a slice of, like, liver pate, [? little round ?] slice.
That was the supper, no bread, nothing.
And when we was inside, that was, we had to be crammed into these beds, these bunk beds, but there is nothing in it.
It was just us.
There's no blanket or anything.
And it was so packed, we had to sleep, you know, head and foot in a way to be able to, to fit into this area.
And that was worse, because it's so hard to lay on.
I don't know which it was worse, to lay on it or be outside standing in the baking sun.
And every so often, I don't know dates and don't know, every so often, we have to go to an area where they was given a shower and a clean rag.
Because it is not call dress.
It's just a rag which always smell very disinfected.
You know, it's almost likely to keep bugs out.
I don't know.
That must be the reason.
But again, they was carry on doing, turn right and left.
We had to go in together and had to come out one by one, turn right, turn left.
And--
But always these, these, these German soldiers, which was absolutely terrible, you know, to-- in front of all these men.
Anyway, later on, I find out the name of it was called Mengele, who I passed when I went in.
I didn't know anything.
I didn't.
And he is the one who was all, when I saw this whatever was going on.
And most likely was with me, I never looked at anybody's face.
I was too frightened.
I used to look at down the ground, you know, told me to turn right, turn left, and that's it.
And so any-- every occasion like that they told which barrack to go to.
And slowly, I recognized when we was free to come out and not need to stand in, they called it tzelappell, to stand.
What that mean, I don't know.
When we didn't need to do that, we was allowed to go to toilet and was allowed to go to the washroom.
I recognized there's less and less crowd what I was seeing before.
And also right and left, the other camps, I seen less and less large number of people.
I did recognize.
And somebody said because people going to go to away from here to work.
So I saw that will be better.
We surely can't be working like this as we were.
And one of these last occasion, men was really-- Auschwitz was getting emptied out.
I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] recognized.
I had to turn right, but my mother didn't.
Your mother was with you at that point?
Yes, yes.
My mother wasn't.
So then I went to this room which they told me to-- the number of the barrack to go into.
It's called barrack, you know, those, those wooden huts.
I recognized that was next to this washroom.
And I thought, if I had to go to the right, and obviously in the right-hand side of the washroom, she might be on the left-hand side of this washroom.
So I-- we wasn't allowed to come out.
But that was, it was a door on the back of this.
The front was locked.
The back wasn't.
I tried it, and I recognized, it's open.
So I still had my own old shoes on, which weren't supposed to be laced up.
If you remember seeing pictures before the war, children had these lace-up shoes.
Was not boots, but the lace-up shoes.
Now I had lost the laces a long time ago, but I still had the shoes on.
And was mud in the area.
And I creeped out.
And I just suddenly, I heard this whistling.
And, and the-- I start to understand German.
I heard the, stop, because I'll shoot.
And I was just running to this washroom.
And I stepped out of my shoes, because the mud stopped.
One of my shoes, I, I stepped out of.
Other one was still on me.
And I running, and I went into one of these-- and I heard that the scream, you know, there's whistling and shouting going on.
And I just run into this washroom.
I went into this, this dustbins-- one of these dustbin, and pulled the lid over me.
And that was the most frightening time of my life, because they came in, and they didn't see me anywhere.
And they was even bang, this-- they bang all these metal dishes, including the one I was in.
And somehow or other, they didn't recognize I was in.
So they were still shouting and whistling.
And then, then they stopped.
And I don't know how long I was there.
But there were, eventually, I heard voices.
People was allowed to come out and go to the washroom.
It's not necessary for washing, for drinking water.
And I heard the voices.
People was came out.
So I thought, it is safe to come out.
And I just didn't know how to come out.
And ended up I moved such a lot, standing atop is tip toe, what this metal dish.
And I couldn't get out, because my legs was all so stiff.
I couldn't get out.
And but then as the top dropped off, people recognized was something going on.
So some women came over and says, what you-- is all Hungarian was there.
What you doing here?
And I said, I was in this to hide away from them.
And I want to be helped to get up.
So they did help me to scramble myself up.
And I staggered into this next block.
And I was starting in the shouting, Mommy, Mommy, [SOBBING] Mrs.
Hochhauser.
And my mother find me, heard.
And she was there.
So we was together again.
And from there, we was taken to working camp, which is called Camp Mauthausen.
Yes, yes.
Yeah?
No.
We were so, it wasn't talking, was not allowed to anyway.
It's only in that washing room with no Germans about to-- you know, there was-- I don't know what we would-- never talk very much.
I'm sure of it in this gray food that we got, it must have been, you know, after the war.
So there must have been some kind of calming something within it, because there was not just women, not just me as a young child.
There was women who had children and got separated from it.
So I'm sure of it, because if they would think seriously, what's happening with them, they will go mad, I think.
I don't know why, but I have a feeling there was something in it to make people very calm.
And of course, women never had period, of course, of this time.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, we met up.
And I can't remember the journey going to-- from Auschwitz to Camp Mauthausen.
But they did say how many of us was taken, not all will be able to work in the same-- accommodated in the same place and work in the same place.
Because there is a small areas, divided into small areas.
So somehow, that felt more comfortable because I thought if we get separated, we are still within the same area.
And that's the place where I got striped uniform-- we all wore-- and a number.
And had the wooden like the, like the Dutch clogs, without being pointed.
And sort of like something like that, and ended up like a boots, laced up boots.
That what we was wearing.
And by then, it was cold.
Do you know what time of year this was you were taken to Mauthausen?
Well, I didn't know, but I got the Red Cross paper which showed the dates.
You remember?
I showed you.
[INAUDIBLE].
It was very cold by then.
And-- but that's all what we had actually, because it was nothing underneath.
It was their trousers and, and jacket, and, and was a hat, as well, you know, sort of hood-like and can be tied under the chin.
And I was go to work in a factory.
We had to walk all the way and back.
And the German soldiers [INAUDIBLE] you know, hurry up.
We always, always says schnell, schnell all of the time.
You know, I had to walk fast.
How you expect them to have energy?
Anyway, they used to have these guard dogs which was snarling.
You know, which was quite frightening as well.
And they had the gun on their shoulder as well.
And so I had to just do what they told.
And at this area, we used to had a plate of soup, which wasn't much.
You know, it's just watery something.
And before work, it was a canteen.
So I think is this [? experience ?] was working during the daytime, because we used to be in work at night.
And that was some men, as well as women, who was coming and going, show us how to do the work and, and then leave us.
But there was always German soldiers.
And was a certain time when we was allowed to go to toilet, with the soldiers taking us and bringing us back to the work.
But my mother wasn't work there.
My mother wasn't work there.
They, they, they, they said they need stronger-- I mean, I was among the not strong-- the stronger people to go to work to snow clearing, clearing snow also at night.
And I was just feel comfortable she's around.
And time to time, she was in that area where I was when we wasn't working.
Because daytime, we were sleeping.
And then once a week, we got-- I don't know once a week or how often, but we had that uniform changed.
Because is, is nothing the cleaning reason, but is, it start getting falling apart because we was in it day and night really, and walking such a distance.
I don't know, very long way.
No, I don't know.
We had no no time to know anything.
And that was going on right through the winter.
It was very cold, the snow.
It was every evening we was going, morning came back.
And was a-- that wasn't as bad, like in Auschwitz, because this, this barracks had a sack like, something to sleep on, which was all crumpled brown paper in it.
So it wasn't quite right on the wood.
You know, it wasn't quite as bad.
And there was some German women working there.
I don't know what they was doing.
It was in, they was in uniform, but they wasn't guarding us.
And I remember one day in, in January, that must have been the time when my mother died.
They told me I can be with them for a week in the nice and warm place.
They was doing, some people was doing writing a lot and other was doing handicraft, you know.
And the handicraft, like making toys and things like that.
And--
I don't know.
But they was in the army, because they had uniform.
But they wasn't doing any guarding of us.
But they was in the, they was like they done some paperwork.
I don't know.
And as I couldn't speak the language, we wasn't talking.
But they was kind to me, because they give me a, a dress to wear, instead of the striped uniform.
And that was come to an end after a few days.
I had to go back again.
And everything I find up later, because I have been, I met a people who was there.
And I find up there was every night, as was no light, it was, because it was war time, it had to be dark.
Every night, as they was shoveling the snow, it had to be constantly, constantly working.
Because they couldn't stop.
It's just like as we was go to work, is nonstop have to keep going, going.
At work, you have to keep going, going there.
Is nonstop.
And then there's these, these German soldiers with the guns and, and all these guard dogs.
People couldn't, couldn't do any other way.
You had to just do as told.
So I had been heard after the war, quite a while after the war, I met up with women who survived.
And they said that area where they was working, there was night time a, a train.
And this, it could not see them until it was too near, too near.
So is every night, some people got killed-- not, not one, several.
So it must have been my mother among those.
Because after the war, she never survived.
But I didn't know.
And anyway, when we came to the end, it was 1945 May, when suddenly recognized, all gates open, no German soldiers.
And we wasn't go to work.
And we was just sitting on the ground because it was quite nice weather.
And we hadn't had very much energy to talk.
We just sit and we haven't been working luckily because we are so tired, just so no energy.
And all this, and we recognized the gate is open.
We didn't know why.
There's no German soldiers.
And all of a sudden, we see the Americans soldiers come in.
We recognized, this is a totally different uniform.
And they was really frightened of us.
They was-- came in and stepped back, back, you know, sort of.
You recognize when they not turning round, just stepping backwards.
Because they was really frighten of us.
I don't know how we was looked like.
Yes, yes.
That was a very large area.
Various work was going on, something to do with wool or cotton wool.
Now, where I was, that area was a great big workroom, huge, with various machines.
And on a conveyor belt on-- great big tubes was coming from other buildings.
Come down, because came in high, and come down on conveyor belt, white cotton wool.
Whether it was for medical reason or what, I don't know.
But this cotton wools was going from this conveyor belt to machines.
And I was standing on one machine, which one was had to handle with the knobs.
One done this, another one that.
So on the top, it was completely covered up in big bales of cotton wools, covered up, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] covered up the-- in a-- and a canvas covers up these big bales of wool.
And somebody else had to put on a wheelbarrow and take it to the storage place.
So I was only, I only know what I was doing.
This was that machine.
And all night-- I was only 13-- all night.
And with that little soup.
I don't know how I had the energy, really.
Don't know.
We went in the evenings and came back in the mornings.
I don't know what time we went, what time we left.
The only good thing was, in this place was hot and cold water.
We could, we drink as much as we like.
And was hot water and we could wash our hair.
Because time till time, these German soldiers went off to eat.
And we was left with the workers.
And they not taken any notice of us.
So if we switched off the machine and then just go in there.
Because by then, a little bit my hair was growing.
And with some liquid soap-- so I just put on my head and washed it, like the others, in turn, you know.
So at least we had warm water, you know.
Well, this was better, was better.
This is, this was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, it wasn't good, but it was a bit better.
Warm water, and we had that clothes, that striped uniform.
At least we was covered.
And had that wooden boots sort on, so our feet was warm.
A work camp.
They, they, they-- yes, these, these Polish ladies were saying, sooner or later whoever still around will go to working camp.
I mean, the Germans must have been recognized they-- they're not going to win the war, so might as well keep the people who was still around to use them for work.
Because most of their men was in uniforms.
So I suppose they used the, the opportunity to do that.
So those people who unfortunately couldn't live, because they were so unwell, the health reason, I mean, they, they finished.
But I have been still survived, so I was working.
You said earlier that you said you were 13 when you were in Auschwitz, you said you were 13 rather than 12.
Yeah.
I really don't know.
And the especially, especially you know, when, when people for days on end wasn't eating properly or wasn't having any, I mean, in the brick factory, you couldn't even wash.
I mean, I suppose there were some thin, short women who wasn't look any different from me.
And then, you know, under these conditions, children's face become so old.
You know, because I remember young children who was looked so old, had sort of [POINTS] lines under their eye.
You know, sort of-- I think people's face changed.
Children's face become different.
You're constantly being frightened.
That make, make children look different.
Yeah.
Yeah.
